text: A four-day workweek boosts productivity per hour worked and improves retention. Companies piloting it have reported equal or higher output with happier teams.
Productivity gains in pilots are cherry-picked. Customer-facing roles cannot compress hours without service gaps, and salaried staff often just shift the same workload into fewer days.
Service gaps are solvable with staggered schedules. The retention effect alone offsets transition costs within a year for most knowledge-work teams.
Retention figures from pilots reflect novelty effects. Once normalised, a compressed week increases burnout for high-stakes roles and shifts costs onto coworkers who absorb the missing day.

The bull case rests on productivity-per-hour and retention gains observed in pilots; the bear case warns about service gaps, redistributed workload and novelty effects. On balance the bull arguments are better evidenced, but the bear case correctly flags that the policy is not universal and depends on role profile. Verdict: cautiously pro four-day week for knowledge work, with case-by-case scrutiny for customer-facing roles.
